stem: Programming languages provide both count-controlled (for) loops and condition-controlled (while) loops.
model_answer: A count-controlled loop repeats a fixed number of times that is known before the loop starts, using a counter variable (e.g. for i = 1 to 10). A condition-controlled loop repeats while (or until) a condition is true, and the number of iterations may not be known in advance (e.g. keep asking for a password until it is correct). Use a count loop when the number of repeats is fixed; use a condition loop when the end depends on data or user input.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Compare count-controlled and condition-controlled iteration, and give an example situation for each.

mark_points.text: count-controlled: repeats a fixed/known number of times (uses a counter)
accepted_alternatives: for loop repeats a set number of times

mark_points.text: condition-controlled: repeats while/until a condition is met; iterations may not be known in advance
accepted_alternatives: while loop runs until condition is false

mark_points.text: example of count-controlled: any situation with a known repeat count (e.g. print 1..10)
accepted_alternatives: printing 10 items / looping through array of fixed size

mark_points.text: example of condition-controlled: any input-validation or until-success situation
accepted_alternatives: repeatedly ask for password until correct
keep reading file lines until end of file
